ground_truth.title: 10 Sci-Fi Shows Every Star Trek Fan Needs To Watch
ground_truth.author: Sam Stone
ground_truth.main_content:
While sci-fi shows had existed before the 'Star Trek' franchise, the original 'Trek' series from the '60s left an indelible mark on the genre that's only endured for decades. But even as 'Star Trek' sees a resurgence on Paramount+, there are still plenty of other sci-fi shows worth a fan's time. The genre has flourished on television in the wake of 'Star Trek: The Original Series' and continues to thrive.

Whether a viewer wants to follow a tight-knit crew cruising the cosmos or learn about some heavy interstellar politics, there are countless 'Star Trek'-adjacent series. Sometimes played for laughs and sometimes deadly serious, these shows have their own legacies with dedicated fans. Audiences looking for something like 'Star Trek' are in luck because there is no shortage of spiritually similar projects to the iconic franchise. Here are 10 sci-fi shows that every 'Star Trek' fan needs to watch to keep those genre thrills coming.

1. Red Dwarf: One of the longest-running sci-fi comedies ever, follows unassuming technician Dave Lister, who is kept in suspended animation for three million years on the spaceship the Red Dwarf. Lister awakens as the last known surviving human in the universe, joined by a holographic program of his bunkmate Arnold Judas Rimmer. This is a sitcom first and foremost, with the genre elements providing inventive ways to keep the gags rolling in.

2. Babylon 5: A story centered on a strategically important space station, set in the 22nd century. As humanity's government regresses into a more totalitarian society, the personnel on Babylon 5 chafe against the change. A very maturely told sci-fi epic, built from the ground-up with a depth in world-building and scale that few other genre properties can match.

3. Stargate SG-1: Expands on the movie's concept of an ancient extraterrestrial portal on Earth providing gateways to other planets. The American military forms Stargate Command to explore the universe, gathering allies and defending against enemies. For a crew-focused, exploration-oriented sci-fi show like many 'Star Trek' series, 'Stargate SG-1' provides the otherworldly thrills.

4. Farscape: Follows astronaut John Crichton, who accidentally is transported to the other side of the cosmos through a wormhole. The definition of a cult classic, 'Farscape' weaved its own wacky sci-fi universe, full of creative extraterrestrial species and galactic dynamics.

5. Firefly: Stars Nathan Fillion as Mal Reynolds, the captain of a small freighter taking jobs on loosely maintained colonies on the fringes of human-settled space. Joss Whedon essentially created a space Western, complete with saloons and isolated, working-class small towns.

6. Battlestar Galactica (2004): A new take on the '70s series, the 2004 reboot opens with humanity standing on the brink of annihilation after a devastating surprise attack by the Cylons. Ronald D. Moore turned it from a campy 'Star Wars' pastiche into a story firmly within the accolades of prestige television.

7. Dark Matter (2015): Follows a group of strangers who emerge from stasis on a traveling spaceship known as the Raza, suffering from amnesia and learning they're spacefaring mercenaries. A twisted subversion of the typical spacefaring crew archetype that 'Star Trek' popularized decades ago.

8. The Expanse: Set in a future where humanity has colonized the solar system, with tension between Earth, Mars, and the asteroid belt colonies. Originally rescued by Prime Video, allowing a satisfying story to unfold. Boasting the talents of Naren Shankar, a former writer for 'Star Trek' shows in the '90s, 'The Expanse' definitely evokes narrative sensibilities in line with 'Deep Space Nine.'

9. The Orville: A clear homage to the early 'Star Trek' series, created by Seth MacFarlane. Like 'Star Trek: The Next Generation,' it improves significantly as it progresses, leaning into emotional development of its main characters without sacrificing laughs. MacFarlane's love of 'Star Trek' is at the forefront of the series.

10. For All Mankind: An alternate history sci-fi show where the Soviet Union beats the United States to land a man on the Moon in 1969. This leads to a prolonged Space Race for decades. Among the first wave of Apple TV original series and one of its longest-running, it provides an engrossing look at what humanity's place in the solar system could've been if the Space Race continued.
with: While sci-fi shows had existed before the 'Star Trek' franchise, the original 'Trek' series from the '60s left an indelible mark on the genre that's only endured for decades.
The genre has flourished on television in the wake of 'Star Trek: The Original Series' and continues to thrive.
Whether a viewer wants to follow a tight-knit crew cruising the cosmos or learn about some heavy interstellar politics, there are countless 'Star Trek'-adjacent series.
Red Dwarf is one of the longest-running sci-fi comedies ever, a British spoof on the genre which started in 1988.
Babylon 5 is a very maturely told sci-fi epic, built from the ground-up with a depth in world-building and scale that few other genre properties can match.
